Import torch, torch.nn as nn, torch.optim, and necessary utilities such as torch.autograd and torch.cuda.amp.autocast
Import the RotaryEmbedding module from rotary_embedding_torch.py
Set a fixed random seed for reproducibility (e.g., torch.manual_seed(42))
Define model and data parameters: embedding dimension dim=64, sequence length seq_len=16, batch size batch_size=2
Instantiate RotaryEmbedding with dim=64, learned_freq=False, freqs_for='lang', use_xpos=False (to use the caching logic in forward), and default other parameters
Create a simple dummy input tensor of shape (batch_size, seq_len, dim) with requires_grad=True, for example torch.randn(batch_size, seq_len, dim, requires_grad=True)
Define a simple linear projection layer (nn.Linear(dim, dim)) to simulate query/key generation from the input embeddings
Define a simple loss function, e.g., mean of the output tensor
In the training loop, perform exactly two iterations as follows:
- For each iteration, zero the gradients of all parameters (optimizer.zero_grad())
- Compute queries and keys by applying the linear layer to the input tensor
- Call rotary_embedding.rotate_queries_with_cached_keys(q, k) to apply rotary embeddings (this will call forward() with caching)
- Compute a scalar loss from the rotated queries (e.g., loss = rotated_q.mean())
- Call loss.backward() without retain_graph
- Call optimizer.step()
After the first iteration, assert no error occurs and gradients are computed
At the second iteration when calling loss.backward(), observe that a RuntimeError is raised: 'Trying to backward through the graph a second time'
Confirm that commenting out caching in rotary_embedding.forward (i.e., disabling use of self.cached_freqs and always computing freqs fresh) removes the error
Optionally, run the same code with huggingface accelerate to confirm similar behavior
Print full stack trace of the RuntimeError to confirm it originates from cached frequency tensors stored during first forward pass being reused in second backward pass
